stories.headline: Wind-driven Dry Creek Fire near Rifle destroys structures, forces evacuations
stories.summary:
A wind-driven wildfire south of Rifle in Garfield County has burned approximately 315 acres and destroyed at least two structures — a home and an outbuilding — since igniting Tuesday afternoon. Evacuation orders remained in effect Wednesday as fire officials warned of extreme fire behavior and strong forecasted winds. The Colorado River Fire Rescue said firefighter and public safety are the top priority, with crews using direct and indirect tactics to slow the fire's spread.

The Upper Colorado River Interagency Fire Management Unit Type 3 Team assumed command from local agencies at 7 a.m. Wednesday. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Crews had worked through the night to protect threatened structures after the fire rapidly spread driven by strong winds to the east and southeast upon their initial arrival.

The blaze is adding to air quality concerns across the Western Slope. Smoke from the Dry Creek fire was expected to be heaviest overnight Wednesday into Thursday morning in the Rifle area, particularly on the southern side of the Colorado River near Garfield County Regional Airport, compounding existing poor air quality from fires in Utah and Nevada.

stories.headline: Tornado warning issued for Morgan County as severe storms pound northeastern Colorado
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a tornado warning for parts of Morgan County on Wednesday after radar indicated a tornado had formed south of Interstate 76 between Fort Morgan and Brush. A broader tornado watch covered northeastern Colorado counties including Adams, Arapahoe, Elbert, Lincoln, Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Sedgwick, Washington and Weld through 11 p.m. Officials warned of softball-sized hail and wind gusts up to 75 mph.

Additional severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for communities along the Front Range, including Loveland, Johnstown and Berthoud, with ping-pong ball-sized hail and winds up to 60 mph possible. Southern Colorado counties including El Paso, Pueblo and Teller were also under a severe thunderstorm watch with potential for 2-inch hail and 70 mph winds. Thunderstorms were expected to remain possible in Denver through Thursday evening.

The storm system follows a previously covered severe weather watch for the Denver metro area. The successive rounds of storms continue to pose threats of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding across the Front Range and Eastern Plains.

stories.headline: Man fatally shot overnight in Denver's Montbello neighborhood
stories.summary:
A man was shot and killed in northeast Denver's Montbello neighborhood early Wednesday morning, according to the Denver Police Department. Officers were first dispatched to the 15000 block of Olmstead Place at 1:49 a.m. after reports of a shooting. The victim was transported to a hospital, where he later died from his injuries.

Denver police classified the death as a homicide as of Wednesday morning. No suspects had been publicly identified or arrested as of Wednesday afternoon. The victim's identity was to be released by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner at a later date.

Anyone with information about the shooting is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Woman charged with menacing, child abuse after brandishing shovel at Silverthorne trailhead parking dispute
stories.summary:
A woman has been arrested on charges of menacing and child abuse after allegedly wielding a 24-inch metal shovel in a threatening manner during a parking dispute at a popular Wildernest trailhead near Silverthorne, according to the Summit County Sheriff's Office. The incident occurred when two women were competing for a parking spot and the suspect blocked the road in both directions before exiting her vehicle with the shovel.

The suspect approached the other driver's vehicle — a mother traveling with her two children — waving the shovel in a threatening manner. The mother called 911 while yelling at the woman to back away. Deputies who responded said the suspect admitted she was trying to scare the other driver away from the parking spot.

The case highlights tensions at overcrowded trailheads in Colorado's mountain communities during the summer season. Authorities remind visitors to have backup plans and to arrive early to avoid parking conflicts at popular recreation areas.

stories.headline: Avalanche trade Jack Drury to Nashville Predators, acquire Svechkov and L'Heureux
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche traded forward Jack Drury to the Nashville Predators on Wednesday in exchange for forwards Fedor Svechkov and Zachary L'Heureux, along with prospect Chase Bradley and a third-round pick in the 2029 NHL Draft. The deal marks the second trade between the two franchises in eight days, following the June 16 acquisition of Ross Colton by Nashville.

Drury, 26, recorded 27 points in 82 regular-season games for Colorado this past season and added five points in 13 playoff games. He was set to become a restricted free agent on July 1. Nashville general manager Chris MacFarland, the former Avalanche GM hired by the Predators on June 2, called Drury a hard-working, elite face-off center capable of handling tough assignments.

Svechkov, 23, had 17 points in 70 games for Nashville this season and is under contract for the next two years. L'Heureux, also 23 and a first-round pick in 2021, contributed five points in 25 games with Nashville. Both players are under two-year contracts beginning next season, giving Colorado a pair of young forwards with NHL experience.

stories.headline: Nuggets pass on 26th pick in NBA Draft; Trevon Brazile selected at No. 35
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets declined to make a selection when their turn came with the 26th overall pick in the NBA Draft on Wednesday night, drawing criticism from some observers who felt the franchise missed an opportunity to add young talent around star Nikola Jokic. The pick passed to another team as Denver remained without a first-round selection.

With the 35th overall pick in the second round, the Nuggets selected Arkansas forward Trevon Brazile. The selection gives Denver a young prospect to develop, though the decision to forego the first-round pick drew scrutiny given questions about the team's depth heading into next season.

The draft decisions come as the Nuggets also face a key offseason question regarding the free agency of forward Peyton Watson. Denver's front office, led by Ben Tenzer and Jon Wallace, faces pressure to build a roster capable of competing for an NBA title alongside Jokic.

stories.headline: Colorado native Wyndham Clark wins second U.S. Open title at Shinnecock Hills
stories.summary:
Denver native Wyndham Clark claimed his second U.S. Open championship Sunday at Shinnecock Hills, shooting a final-round 73 to hold on to a one-shot lead and capture the title. The victory adds to Clark's 2023 U.S. Open win, making him one of only a handful of players to win multiple U.S. Open titles in the modern era.

Clark's final round was described as full of ups and downs before he held on for the victory. The Colorado native has become one of the marquee figures in professional golf, with his continued success on major stages elevating his national profile.

The win is a significant moment for Colorado golf and marks another chapter in Clark's rapid rise since his breakthrough major victory three years ago.

stories.headline: Denver-based Birdcall chicken sandwich chain plans expansion of up to 85 Midwest locations by 2031
stories.summary:
Birdcall, a Denver-based chicken sandwich chain, has announced plans to open up to 85 franchised locations across Indiana, Kansas, Missouri, Nebraska, Ohio and Wisconsin by 2031 as part of a broader national growth strategy. The expansion represents a significant move for the regional chain, which has built its brand in the Colorado market before setting its sights on the Midwest.

The franchise push would significantly multiply Birdcall's footprint beyond its Colorado roots. The company's growth plans come amid a competitive fast-casual chicken sandwich market dominated by national chains.

The announcement signals growing confidence among Denver-based food and beverage brands in pursuing national expansion. Birdcall's Midwest push follows the broader trend of Colorado restaurant concepts scaling into new regional markets.

stories.headline: Avenues at Northfield launches multimillion-dollar redevelopment with pedestrian mall, Wayfair anchor
stories.summary:
The Shops at Northfield, recently rebranded as the Avenues at Northfield, has launched a multimillion-dollar redevelopment centered on transforming its Main Street into a pedestrian-only walkway. Plans include a central lawn, shaded gathering areas, mature trees, children's play spaces, fire and water features, outdoor dining patios and year-round programming such as concerts and markets at the 1.1 million-square-foot property in Denver's Central Park neighborhood.

Furniture retailer Wayfair is set to open a 140,000-square-foot brick-and-mortar store at the site by late this year, occupying the former Macy's building at 8298 E. Northfield Blvd. The location will employ approximately 150 people and will be Wayfair's third large-format physical store, following openings in Illinois and Atlanta.

Los Angeles-based Stockdale Capital Partners, which acquired the center in 2022, is driving the renovation. The Central Park development attracts more than 8.5 million visitors annually and sits on the former grounds of Stapleton International Airport. Updated storefronts, enhanced lighting, signage and landscaping are being rolled out across the property.

stories.headline: Collared Colorado wolf crosses east of I-25 for first time since 2023 reintroduction
stories.summary:
A collared wolf has crossed east of Interstate 25 for the first time since wolves were reintroduced to Colorado in 2023, Colorado Parks and Wildlife announced Wednesday. The wolf briefly crossed the highway corridor in southeastern Colorado and moved through watersheds in Pueblo, Otero and Las Animas counties before returning west of the interstate, according to the agency's monthly activity map.

CPW said the movement highlights the wide-ranging dispersal behavior of Colorado's growing wolf population. Officials said they are in active communication with livestock producers in areas with known wolf activity and are coordinating access to conflict-minimization resources.

Wolves also remained active near Gunnison, Rifle, Meeker, Vail and Steamboat Springs, as well as the San Luis Valley and along the New Mexico border, according to the latest map. At least 31 wolves are now documented in Colorado, though not all animals wear GPS collars that transmit their locations.

stories.headline: Bret Saunders, KBCO morning personality for nearly 30 years, abruptly leaves station amid iHeartMedia cuts
stories.summary:
Bret Saunders, the longtime morning drive personality at Boulder's KBCO 97.3, announced his departure from the station Wednesday after nearly 28 years, stunning a large and loyal audience across the Denver-Boulder metro. Saunders posted on X that his time at KBCO had come to an end, thanking listeners for being part of his life without providing further explanation. Representatives of iHeartMedia, which owns KBCO, did not respond to requests for comment.

The departure appears to be part of broader staffing cuts at iHeartMedia's Denver operations. In April, iHeart Denver Region President Brenda Eggers was laid off, and on Wednesday, 106.7 The Bull personality Denise LaPlante also announced she had been let go after eight years with the company. Saunders had indicated in an April social media post that he had recently re-signed with KBCO, making the sudden departure surprising to many followers.

Saunders began his Colorado radio career in Craig before moving to Fort Collins' KTCL in 1993, then joining KBCO in 1997. He developed close relationships with prominent musicians over the decades, including the Lumineers, whose lead singer Wesley Schultz credited Saunders as the band's earliest and biggest radio supporter. Saunders said he would check in with fans again soon.

stories.headline: Democratic governor's race pits U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet against Attorney General Phil Weiser ahead of June 30 primary
stories.summary:
Colorado's Democratic primary for governor, set for June 30, features a contest between U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet and state Attorney General Phil Weiser. Bennet has served in the U.S. Senate since 2009, when he was appointed by Gov. Bill Ritter, and previously served as managing director of the Anschutz Investment Company and as chief of staff to then-Denver Mayor John Hickenlooper. Weiser has served as attorney general since 2019, previously clerking for U.S. Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg and serving in the Obama administration's Department of Justice.

The race is one of several competitive Democratic primaries on the June 30 ballot, which also includes contests for U.S. Senate and other statewide offices. Colorado Newsline has published a voter guide with candidate questionnaire responses to help voters compare the two candidates ahead of next week's election.

The outcome of the Democratic gubernatorial primary will determine who faces the Republican nominee in the November general election for the open seat. Both candidates have significant name recognition and fundraising bases across the state.

weather.notable: About 13 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: There should be a children's song: 'If you're happy and you know it, keep it to yourself and let your dad sleep.'

topStory.headline: Twin Earthquakes Kill at Least 164 in Venezuela; Acting President Declares State of Emergency
topStory.summary:
Two powerful earthquakes struck Venezuela in rapid succession Wednesday evening, killing at least 164 people and injuring more than 971 others, according to Acting President Delcy Rodríguez. The first quake, measured at magnitude 7.2, hit near the town of San Felipe roughly 100 miles west of the capital, Caracas, at approximately 6 p.m. ET. A second, larger quake measuring magnitude 7.5 struck 39 seconds later near the nearby town of Yumare. The U.S. Geological Survey described the event as likely causing widespread casualties and extensive damage, calling it among the strongest earthquakes to hit Venezuela in more than a century.

Rodríguez declared a state of emergency and announced the closure of Caracas's main airport due to heavy structural damage. She said more than 100 aftershocks had been recorded and described the state of La Guaira, north of Caracas, as a "true tragedy" and a "disaster zone," noting that casualty figures from that region were not yet included in the official death toll. Footage circulating on social media showed multiple apartment buildings leveled along a coastal road in Caraballeda, in La Guaira state, with NBC News estimating at least eight apartment blocks destroyed within a stretch of less than half a mile. Rodríguez announced a $200 million fund for reconstruction of infrastructure and housing.

The earthquakes struck on a national holiday marking the 205th anniversary of the 1821 Battle of Carabobo, a decisive engagement that secured Venezuelan independence from Spanish royalist forces. Because the holiday had left many residents at home rather than at work, the potential for civilian casualties was heightened. Seismologist Lucy Jones of the California Institute of Technology described the combination of a high-magnitude quake and dense residential areas as particularly destructive.

International assistance began mobilizing within hours of the disaster. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the immediate deployment of search and rescue teams, medical resources, and humanitarian aid. President Donald Trump said he had instructed all government agencies to prepare to move quickly, describing Venezuela as a "new and great" friend of the United States. Rodríguez said she spoke with Rubio by phone and thanked the U.S. government for maintaining "permanent contact" with Venezuelan authorities. China, Argentina, Brazil, and Mexico also pledged support.

The United Nations and the International Red Cross separately mobilized response teams. UN Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs Tom Fletcher warned that the disaster "risks deepening existing vulnerabilities," noting that eight million people in Venezuela were already in need of humanitarian assistance before the earthquakes struck. The Red Cross identified search and rescue, emergency shelter, healthcare, safe water, and sanitation as the most urgent anticipated needs in the coming days.
